content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

One credential bypasses every security rule you’ve ever written. In April 2026, a popular AI coding platform showed exactly how easily that credential ends up somewhere it should never be.
Every Supabase project ships with two keys, and the gap between them is the difference between a regular visitor and the database owner.
The anon key is the one meant for your browser. It carries no special privilege of its own. Whatever a user can do with it is entirely decided by the Row-Level Security policies you’ve written, table by table, rule by rule. Spend two hours writing those policies correctly, and the anon key respects every line of them.
The service role key is a different category of thing entirely. It carries what Postgres calls the BYPASSRLS attribute. Every policy you wrote, every rule about who can see what, gets skipped automatically the moment a request authenticates with this key. It was built for one purpose: backend jobs, migrations, and admin tools that legitimately need to touch every row in every table, without asking permission from the rules meant for regular users.
The problem is that nothing about using this key feels dangerous in the moment. It just works, immediately, without the friction of writing a single policy. And that convenience is exactly why it keeps ending up somewhere it was never supposed to go.
What actually happened in April
On April 20, 2026, Lovable, one of the more widely used AI app-building platforms, disclosed a specific incident. For roughly ten weeks, from early February through that disclosure date, project chat histories and generated source code on the platform could be read by other users of the platform. Any credential a builder had pasted into a chat during that window, service role keys included, alongside third-party keys for services like Stripe or SendGrid, needed to be treated as compromised.
The technical fix for an individual affected project is almost embarrassingly small. Rotate the key. It takes about ten seconds in a dashboard. The part actually worth sitting with is how the key got into that exposure window in the first place, which is a pattern showing up constantly across AI-assisted development right now: a builder pastes a credential into a chat interface to get help debugging something, and that credential now lives somewhere outside their direct control.
A separate audit of one hundred vibe-coded applications, conducted independently of this incident, found that forty-one percent had exposed secrets or API keys somewhere in the codebase. Twenty-one percent had no authentication on their API endpoints at all. Twelve percent had Supabase credentials sitting directly readable inside the compiled frontend JavaScript bundle, meaning anyone who opened their browser’s developer tools and searched the page source would find them.
The gap most builders don’t know exists
Here’s the detail that catches almost everyone off guard the first time they hear it. Row-Level Security only gets enabled automatically when you create a table through Supabase’s Table Editor interface in the browser. Create that same table through a raw SQL migration, or let an AI coding tool generate it for you directly against the database, and RLS is off by default. Nobody flips a switch to turn security off. It simply never gets turned on, because the table was never built through the one specific path that enables it automatically.
Combine that gap with a service role key sitting somewhere it shouldn’t be, and you get the exact failure mode security researchers now describe as the single most common critical issue in Supabase-backed applications: tables fully exposed through the public API, readable and writable by anyone holding even the unprivileged anon key, because no policy was ever attached to restrict access in the first place.
The part that goes further than a leaked credential
There’s a second layer to this problem that’s stranger and considerably harder to guard against with a simple rotation.
Security researcher Simon Willison documented a pattern in 2025 involving AI coding assistants connected to a database through an MCP server configured with service-role-level access. In that setup, the AI has moved well past being a tool a developer uses to write code. It has direct, standing access to query and modify the actual database, with every RLS policy bypassed by design. Willison showed that a malicious value sitting inside a normal database row, something as mundane as a user’s submitted text field, could contain instructions that the AI assistant would read and act on the next time it queried that table, potentially executing SQL the developer never asked for, including dropping tables or pulling data out of the system entirely.
The threat here has nothing to do with a human pasting a key somewhere risky. It’s the AI’s own elevated access being turned against the database by content already sitting inside it, a fundamentally different threat model than simply failing to commit secrets carefully. The mitigation is narrow and specific: never configure an AI assistant’s database access at the service-role level. Whatever access you give the model should be scoped exactly the way you’d scope access for an unpredictable intern, not a database owner.
What actually needs to change
None of this requires abandoning Supabase or AI-assisted development. It requires treating a small number of specific facts as non-negotiable.
The service role key never touches client-side code, ever, under any circumstance, including quick debugging sessions where pasting it into a chat interface feels harmless. Every table your application creates gets checked for RLS explicitly, especially any table that came from a migration file or an AI-generated script rather than the Table Editor UI. Any AI tool with direct database access gets scoped to the least privilege that still lets it do its job, never the service role. And any credential that has ever touched a third-party chat interface, tool, or platform gets rotated the moment there’s any doubt about where that platform’s data actually lives.
The gap between the anon key and the service role key is really the gap between a visitor and an owner. Most of the security incidents showing up in AI-built applications right now trace back to that distinction getting blurred, usually by accident, usually in the name of moving fast enough to ship.
I cover this exact failure mode, along with the broader pattern of AI-generated applications shipping without the access controls their builders assumed were already there, in Vibe. Ship. Scale. The policies you write are only as good as the key that’s allowed to ignore them.
